title: Electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation blends significant hands-on physical work (assembling prototypes, calibrating equipment, soldering, physical testing, site visits) with knowledge-based tasks (writing reports, analyzing test data, drafting specifications) that AI can assist with. AI tools can increasingly help with design analysis, report writing, and simulation, but the core physical tasks of building, testing, and repairing hardware in labs and factories create a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement. The BLS already notes that automation of manual tasks is limiting growth, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI exposure.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
exposure_rationale: Electrical and electronics engineers do substantial knowledge work — design, simulation, documentation, analysis — that is increasingly assisted by AI tools (e.g., AI-aided circuit design, simulation optimization, code generation for embedded systems). However, a meaningful portion of the job involves physical inspection, lab testing, site visits, hands-on troubleshooting, and supervising manufacturing, which creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement. AI is likely to make individual engineers significantly more productive (accelerating design iteration, schematic generation, and documentation) but the deep domain expertise, physical validation requirements, and safety-critical judgment needed mean the occupation faces restructuring rather than elimination.

title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally hands-on and physical — workers must physically inspect, disassemble, repair, and test electrical/electronic equipment in factories, substations, vehicles, and job sites. AI can assist with diagnostics, fault pattern recognition, and documentation, but cannot replace the physical manipulation, on-site judgment, and manual dexterity required. The core work product is a repaired physical system, not a digital artifact, which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation.

title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and field-based, requiring workers to climb poles and towers, string cables, operate heavy equipment, and work in hazardous outdoor environments — tasks AI cannot perform. AI might assist with outage diagnostics, route optimization, or monitoring systems, but the core work of physically installing and repairing power lines remains firmly in the human domain. The strong projected job growth further reflects that this work cannot be meaningfully automated by AI.

title: Electricians
exposure_rationale: Electricians perform highly physical, hands-on work that requires on-site presence, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable environments. AI might assist with blueprint reading, code lookups, or scheduling, but the core tasks—running wire, installing systems, troubleshooting physical faults—cannot be automated by AI. Strong job growth projections further confirm this occupation is insulated from AI displacement.

title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly hands-on and physical, involving operating machinery, soldering, calibrating equipment, repairing hydraulic/pneumatic systems, and working at industrial sites — tasks that AI cannot directly perform. AI may assist with documentation, test result analysis, and troubleshooting diagnostics, but the core work requires physical dexterity, on-site presence, and manual skill. The BLS itself notes automation has mixed effects, still requiring humans to operate and maintain robotic equipment.

title: Elementary, middle, and high school principals
exposure_rationale: School principals do substantial knowledge work (data analysis, reporting, scheduling, budgeting) that AI can assist with, but the core of the role is deeply interpersonal — managing staff, counseling students, engaging parents, and maintaining school culture — requiring physical presence and human judgment in unpredictable environments. AI tools may improve efficiency on administrative tasks but cannot replace the leadership, relationship-building, and real-time decision-making that define the job. The role's authority and accountability are inherently human-centered, limiting how much AI can restructure the occupation.

title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to install, maintain, and repair complex mechanical and electrical systems in buildings. While AI might assist with diagnostics, troubleshooting guidance, or maintenance record-keeping, the core work — working in elevator shafts, connecting wiring, replacing mechanical components, and performing physical inspections — cannot be automated by AI. The job requires physical presence in unpredictable environments, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving with heavy equipment.

title: Emergency management directors
exposure_rationale: Emergency management directors do substantial knowledge work — planning, analysis, writing reports, coordinating information — that AI can meaningfully assist with, but the core of the job involves real-time crisis leadership, interpersonal coordination across agencies, and on-the-ground decision-making under stress that requires human judgment and physical presence. AI tools could enhance planning, damage assessment, and information synthesis, but the irreducibly human elements of commanding emergency operations, building trust with community stakeholders, and making high-stakes decisions in chaotic environments limit how much AI can displace. The role's heavy reliance on experience, relationships, and situational authority makes full automation implausible.

title: EMTs and paramedics
exposure_rationale: EMT and paramedic work is fundamentally physical and requires real-time human presence in unpredictable, high-stakes environments — responding to emergencies, physically treating and transporting patients, and performing hands-on medical procedures. AI may assist peripherally with documentation, dispatch optimization, or diagnostic support tools, but cannot replace the core physical and interpersonal demands of the job. The unpredictable nature of emergency scenes and the irreplaceable need for human judgment and physical intervention create strong natural barriers to AI displacement.

title: Entertainment and recreation managers
exposure_rationale: Entertainment and recreation managers split their time between administrative/planning tasks (scheduling, budgeting, strategy) that AI can assist with and hands-on, people-facing work (engaging customers, supervising staff, inspecting facilities, managing live events) that requires physical presence and interpersonal judgment. AI tools can meaningfully help with scheduling, budget analysis, customer feedback analysis, and communications, but the core value of the role lies in leadership, relationship management, and real-time operational oversight in physical settings. The strong projected job growth and inherently social/physical nature of the work limit AI's ability to substantially reduce headcount.

title: Environmental engineering technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of field/lab work (collecting samples, operating equipment, inspecting sites, handling hazardous materials) and knowledge work (reviewing documents, maintaining records, analyzing data). AI can meaningfully assist with the data analysis, documentation, and compliance review portions, but the core physical tasks — fieldwork, sample collection, equipment operation, and on-site inspections — require human presence and cannot be automated by AI. The BLS itself notes that automated environmental testing may limit employment growth, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI/automation impact.

title: Environmental engineers
exposure_rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial knowledge work — report writing, data analysis, regulatory compliance, and design — that AI can meaningfully assist with, pushing this toward high exposure. However, a significant portion of the job involves site inspections, fieldwork, physical system design requiring professional licensure, and coordination with diverse stakeholders in real-world settings, which limits full AI displacement. AI tools can accelerate report generation, data analysis, and regulatory research, but the combination of physical presence requirements, professional judgment, and PE-licensed sign-off authority keeps this from reaching the very high exposure tier.

title: Environmental science and protection technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation combines significant fieldwork (collecting samples, conducting inspections, operating monitoring equipment outdoors) with knowledge-based tasks (data analysis, report writing, compliance verification). AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and reporting components, but the core fieldwork—physical sample collection, site inspections, and hands-on equipment operation—requires human presence and cannot be automated. The mix of physical/field duties and office/lab work places this squarely in the moderate exposure range.

title: Environmental scientists and specialists
exposure_rationale: Environmental scientists do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, report writing, regulatory compliance, and research synthesis — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, a significant portion of the job involves fieldwork (sample collection, site assessments), client interaction, and regulatory judgment that requires physical presence and contextual human expertise. AI tools can accelerate literature review, data analysis, and report generation, making individual scientists more productive, but the blend of field-based and office-based work creates a natural ceiling on full automation.

title: Epidemiologists
exposure_rationale: Epidemiologists do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, statistical modeling, report writing, literature review, and grant writing — all areas where AI can provide significant assistance and productivity gains. However, the role also involves fieldwork, community engagement, study design requiring expert judgment, and navigating complex public health systems, which limits full automation. AI tools are already transforming data analysis and pattern recognition in epidemiology, but the scientific judgment, stakeholder communication, and policy translation components still require human expertise.

title: Exercise physiologists
exposure_rationale: Exercise physiologists combine hands-on client interaction, physical assessment, and in-person exercise program implementation with knowledge-based tasks like analyzing medical histories and designing treatment plans. AI can assist with data analysis, program design recommendations, and record-keeping, but the core work involves direct physical presence with clients, real-time monitoring, and interpersonal therapeutic relationships that are difficult to automate. The predominantly self-employed, client-facing nature of the work and the need for physical assessment and supervision create meaningful barriers to AI displacement.

title: Farmers, ranchers, and other agricultural managers
exposure_rationale: Farming and ranch management is predominantly a physical, outdoor occupation requiring hands-on work with land, crops, livestock, and machinery in unpredictable natural environments. While AI can assist with some peripheral tasks like market analysis, weather monitoring, precision agriculture planning, and financial record-keeping, the core job involves physical presence, mechanical skills, and real-time environmental judgment that AI cannot replace. The occupation already faces decline due to mechanization and consolidation, but this is driven by physical automation (machinery) rather than AI-driven digital transformation.

title: Fashion designers
exposure_rationale: Fashion design involves significant digital work (CAD programs, trend research, digital sketching) that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, including generating design concepts, predicting trends, and creating virtual prototypes. However, the job also requires substantial physical and tactile elements — handling fabrics, working with models, attending trade shows, and exercising highly subjective creative judgment rooted in cultural intuition and personal artistic vision. AI can augment productivity meaningfully but the creative, relational, and physical dimensions of the role provide a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
exposure_rationale: Film and video editing is almost entirely computer-based work involving organizing, cutting, and assembling digital footage — tasks where AI tools (automated editing, scene detection, color grading, audio syncing) are advancing rapidly and can already handle significant portions of the workflow. Camera operators have more physical, on-location work that creates a natural barrier to AI automation, though robotic cameras and drone technology are already reducing some roles. The split nature of this occupation — highly digital editing work combined with physical camera operation — lands it in the high exposure range, with editors facing greater displacement risk than operators.

title: Kindergarten and elementary school teachers
exposure_rationale: Elementary teaching involves significant physical presence, real-time human interaction, and relationship-building with young children that AI cannot replicate — supervising recess, managing classroom behavior, and nurturing social-emotional development are inherently human tasks. However, AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, grading, generating instructional materials, and personalizing learning content, making teachers more productive in their knowledge-work tasks. The core of the job remains anchored in physical presence and interpersonal care for children, limiting AI's ability to fully restructure the occupation even as it becomes a useful tool.

title: Labor relations specialists
exposure_rationale: Labor relations specialists do substantial knowledge work — drafting contracts, analyzing labor law, writing policies, researching grievances — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the core of the job involves high-stakes interpersonal negotiation, dispute resolution, and building trust between adversarial parties, which requires human judgment and relationship skills that AI cannot replicate. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level where AI tools will boost individual productivity (e.g., contract drafting, compliance research) but the irreducibly human elements of mediation and negotiation provide a meaningful buffer.

title: Landscape architects
exposure_rationale: Landscape architecture is a mix of digital knowledge work (CADD design, GIS analysis, cost estimation, client presentations, environmental analysis) and physical site work (inspections, material selection, on-site coordination). AI tools can significantly assist with the design, visualization, and analytical components — generative design, automated drafting, environmental modeling — potentially making individual architects much more productive. However, the job retains meaningful physical presence requirements, creative judgment about natural environments, and client relationship management that limit full automation.

title: Lawyers
exposure_rationale: Legal work is predominantly knowledge-based — research, document drafting, contract review, and legal analysis — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already demonstrating strong capability. However, core lawyering involves significant human elements: courtroom advocacy, client relationships, negotiation, and the exercise of judgment in high-stakes, nuanced situations that still require human presence and accountability. The BLS itself notes that 'some routine legal work may be automated,' and AI tools like contract review, legal research, and document generation are already reshaping the profession, but the irreducibly interpersonal and adversarial nature of legal practice provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
exposure_rationale: Librarians do substantial knowledge work — research assistance, database management, collection curation, and information organization — that AI can increasingly handle, and AI-powered search tools directly threaten the core reference and research-assistance functions. However, significant portions of the role involve physical presence (managing physical collections, running community programs, working with patrons face-to-face, supervising staff), and the job has strong institutional and community-service dimensions that resist full automation. The slow projected job growth suggests AI and digital resources are already suppressing demand, but the interpersonal, educational, and community-programming aspects provide meaningful insulation.

title: Library technicians and assistants
exposure_rationale: Library technicians and assistants perform a mix of physical tasks (reshelving books, managing physical materials) and information-processing tasks (cataloguing, answering reference questions, maintaining databases) that AI can increasingly handle. However, significant physical presence, patron interaction, and community programming roles provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Licensed practical and licensed vocational nurses

However, the sales, relationship-building, and client-guidance components (especially for complex commercial and mortgage loans) provide some buffer, keeping this from the highest exposure tier.

title: Lodging managers
exposure_rationale: Lodging managers split their time between physical presence tasks (inspecting rooms, greeting guests, resolving on-site problems, supervising staff) and knowledge work (budgeting, scheduling, revenue management, communications). The occupation is unlikely to be heavily restructured by AI, though productivity tools may reduce the need for some administrative support roles around it.

title: Logging workers

title: Logisticians
exposure_rationale: Logisticians do predominantly knowledge work — analyzing supply chains, forecasting demand, coordinating procurement, and managing data systems — all of which are areas where AI tools can meaningfully augment productivity. AI can automate routine optimization, demand forecasting, and data analysis tasks, but the liaison, negotiation, and adaptive problem-solving dimensions provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Machinists and tool and die makers

However, a meaningful portion of their work involves digital tasks like CAD/CAM programming, CNC code generation, and blueprint interpretation, where AI tools are increasingly capable and already contributing to efficiency gains. The projected employment decline, particularly for tool and die makers (-11%), reflects automation's growing role, but the irreducible physical presence and tactile skill requirements keep overall AI exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Management analysts
exposure_rationale: Management analysts do predominantly knowledge work — gathering data, analyzing financials, developing recommendations, and writing reports — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already providing substantial productivity gains. However, a significant portion of the job involves client-facing interpersonal work: interviewing personnel, conducting onsite observations, building trust with managers, and navigating organizational politics, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI substitution. AI will likely make individual analysts far more productive (accelerating data analysis, report writing, and proposal generation), potentially reducing headcount needed per engagement, but the relationship-driven and judgment-intensive consulting role will persist in some form.

title: Manicurists and pedicurists

AI might marginally assist with scheduling or business administration for salon owners, but this represents a negligible fraction of the job.

While human judgment is still needed for strategic interpretation and client communication, the core analytical and reporting tasks are highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation.

AI is likely to accelerate research workflows and reduce the number of support staff needed, but the experimental, physical, and regulatory dimensions of the role preserve substantial human involvement.

title: Medical transcriptionists

The core work product is fully digital with no physical component, and AI can already perform the primary task autonomously, with humans increasingly relegated to editing AI-generated drafts rather than doing original transcription.

title: Meeting, convention, and event planners
exposure_rationale: Event planning involves a significant mix of digital knowledge work (budgeting, communications, vendor research, contract drafting) where AI can meaningfully assist, and substantial physical/interpersonal components (site inspections, on-site coordination, relationship-building with clients and vendors, real-time problem-solving during events) that resist automation. AI tools can streamline planning documents, vendor comparisons, and communications, but the core value of the role lies in human judgment, negotiation, and managing complex interpersonal dynamics in physical settings. The job is unlikely to be heavily restructured by AI given its inherently relational and logistical nature, though productivity gains from AI assistance are plausible.

AI's role is more as a driver of industrial automation (a longer-standing trend) than as a direct cognitive replacement, placing this in the low-to-moderate exposure range.

title: Microbiologists

title: Middle school teachers

title: Military careers

AI can assist with intelligence analysis, logistics planning, and administrative tasks, but the core of military service involves physical presence, real-world decision-making under life-or-death conditions, and human leadership that AI cannot replace. Some specialty roles (e.g., intelligence analysts, administrative officers) face higher exposure, but the overall occupation as defined here remains predominantly physical and interpersonal.

title: Mining and geological engineers
exposure_rationale: Mining and geological engineers split their time between knowledge work (designing mines, preparing technical reports, analyzing data, planning operations) and physical site work (supervising construction, inspecting mines, monitoring equipment). The combination of complex engineering judgment, regulatory responsibility, and field work creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement, placing this occupation in the moderate-to-high exposure range.

title: Models

The creative and artistic dimensions of both roles, combined with the human relationships and live performance aspects, provide some buffer, but the digital/compositional side of the work faces meaningful AI disruption.

title: Musicians and singers
exposure_rationale: Musicians and singers are fundamentally tied to physical performance, live presence, and human artistic expression, which creates a natural barrier to AI automation of the core job. However, AI does pose meaningful indirect threats: AI-generated music can substitute for session musicians and background tracks, AI tools can assist with composition and production, and streaming algorithms already affect how music is discovered and monetized. The live performance and human connection aspects remain resilient, but the recording/studio side of the profession faces real disruption.

title: Natural sciences managers

AI will likely make these managers more productive in their administrative and analytical tasks, but the supervisory, relational, and domain-expert judgment components provide a meaningful buffer against displacement.

title: Semiconductor processing technicians
exposure_rationale: Semiconductor processing technicians work primarily in physical cleanroom environments, operating and monitoring specialized manufacturing equipment, handling silicon wafers, and performing hands-on quality control tasks that require physical presence and dexterity. While AI can assist with process monitoring, data analysis, and defect detection, the core job involves physical manipulation of equipment and materials in a highly controlled environment that AI cannot replace. Some peripheral tasks like reviewing process data and documentation have modest AI exposure, but the fundamentally physical and equipment-intensive nature of the work limits overall AI impact.

title: Set and exhibit designers
exposure_rationale: Set and exhibit designers do substantial computer-based work including CAD design, drafting, budgeting, and visual planning, all areas where AI tools are rapidly improving and can meaningfully augment productivity. However, the job also requires significant physical presence (overseeing installations, traveling with shows), hands-on collaboration with directors and curators, and creative spatial judgment that remains difficult to fully automate. AI can accelerate the design iteration and visualization phases but cannot replace the on-site coordination, physical problem-solving, and interpersonal collaboration that are central to the role.

Utilizing spatial measurement and site mapping for physical layouts.

title: Sheet metal workers
exposure_rationale: Sheet metal work is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and on-site presence to fabricate, install, and maintain metal products. While some fabrication tasks are already automated via CNC machinery and CADD systems, the core installation and maintenance work requires human workers in unpredictable physical environments. AI may assist with design, planning, or equipment programming at the margins, but cannot replace the physical labor of bending, cutting, welding, climbing, and fitting sheet metal in construction settings.

title: Skincare specialists
exposure_rationale: Skincare specialists perform hands-on physical treatments — facials, hair removal, massages, peels — that require direct human touch and in-person client interaction, creating a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI might assist peripherally with product recommendations, scheduling, or skin analysis tools, but cannot replicate the manual dexterity and physical presence central to the job. The occupation's projected growth further reflects its resilience as a service requiring human contact.

title: Small engine mechanics
exposure_rationale: Small engine mechanics perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — disassembling engines, replacing parts, using hand and pneumatic tools — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity in ways AI cannot replicate. AI could assist with diagnostics, repair documentation, or parts lookup, but these are peripheral to the core job. The work is fundamentally tactile and location-dependent, placing it firmly in the low-exposure category.

title: Social and community service managers
exposure_rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (data analysis, grant writing, program evaluation, budget management) with substantial interpersonal and community-facing responsibilities that require human presence and judgment. AI can meaningfully assist with writing proposals, analyzing program effectiveness data, and administrative tasks, but core functions like supervising staff, community outreach, stakeholder relationship management, and navigating complex human service situations resist automation. The mix of office-based knowledge work and direct human interaction places this occupation in the moderate exposure range.

title: Social and human service assistants
exposure_rationale: This role is heavily centered on in-person human interaction, relationship-building, and navigating complex social situations with vulnerable populations — tasks that require empathy, trust, and physical presence that AI cannot replicate. While AI could assist with administrative tasks like paperwork, researching available benefits, or coordinating services, the core value of the job lies in interpersonal skills and community-based case management. The occupation's projected growth and its dependence on human judgment in sensitive, unpredictable situations further limit AI's ability to meaningfully displace these workers.

title: Social workers
exposure_rationale: Social work is fundamentally built on human relationships, empathy, and in-person interaction with vulnerable populations — qualities that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with administrative tasks like case documentation, resource referrals, and record-keeping, and could potentially support assessment and triage functions. The core therapeutic, advocacy, and crisis-response work requires physical presence, emotional attunement, and trust-based relationships that create a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

title: Sociologists
exposure_rationale: Sociologists do predominantly knowledge work — designing research, analyzing data, writing reports, and developing policy recommendations — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and can provide substantial productivity gains. AI tools can already assist significantly with literature reviews, statistical analysis, survey design, and report writing, meaning fewer sociologists may be needed to produce the same output. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as conducting interviews, building rapport with research subjects, exercising nuanced judgment about social phenomena, and collaborating with policymakers, which provide some buffer against full automation.

title: Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers
exposure_rationale: Software development is almost entirely digital knowledge work — writing code, designing systems, analyzing requirements, and testing — all domains where AI is advancing rapidly and already providing substantial productivity gains through tools like GitHub Copilot, Claude, and GPT-4. AI can automate significant portions of coding, test generation, debugging, and documentation, meaning fewer developers may be needed to produce the same output, and QA/testing roles face particular disruption as automated AI-driven testing matures. The score is 8 rather than 9-10 because the job still requires substantial human judgment in architecture decisions, stakeholder communication, creative problem-solving, and navigating complex organizational contexts that AI cannot yet fully replicate.

title: Solar photovoltaic installers
exposure_rationale: Solar PV installation is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring workers to climb roofs, handle heavy equipment, cut and assemble structural components, and perform electrical connections on-site — tasks AI cannot perform. AI might assist with system design, configuration planning, or scheduling at the periphery, but the core work is irreducibly physical and location-dependent. The occupation's strong projected growth further reflects that demand is driven by physical installation needs that cannot be digitized.

title: Special education teachers
exposure_rationale: Special education teaching is fundamentally a human-presence job requiring physical interaction, emotional attunement, and individualized relationship-building with students who have disabilities — core aspects that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with administrative tasks like drafting IEPs, lesson planning, progress tracking, and adapting curriculum materials, which represent a significant portion of the workload. The job's irreducible need for in-person instruction, behavioral management, and trust-based relationships with vulnerable students creates a strong natural barrier to deeper AI displacement.

title: Special effects artists and animators
exposure_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely computer-based and digital, with core tasks involving creating graphics, animation, and visual effects using software — all domains where AI is advancing rapidly with tools like generative AI image/video synthesis, AI-assisted animation, and procedural generation. The BLS itself notes that AI may dampen demand by automating routine animation and special effects tasks. While high-end creative judgment, artistic vision, and client collaboration still require human skill, the trajectory of AI in this field is steep and the slow projected job growth (2%) likely understates disruption as AI tools make individual artists dramatically more productive.

title: Speech-language pathologists
exposure_rationale: Speech-language pathology is fundamentally a hands-on clinical profession requiring direct patient interaction, physical assessment of swallowing and articulation, and therapeutic relationship-building that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, treatment planning, diagnostic support, and some teletherapy applications. The strong projected job growth (15%) reflects genuine human demand that AI is unlikely to displace, as the core therapeutic work requires physical presence, nuanced listening, and compassionate human engagement.

title: Stationary engineers and boiler operators
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to operate, inspect, maintain, and repair boilers and mechanical equipment in person. While AI and automation can assist with monitoring gauges and logging data, the core tasks — physical inspection, manual repairs, troubleshooting equipment failures, and operating valves and controls — require physical presence and dexterity that AI cannot replicate. The slow projected employment growth reflects gradual automation of monitoring functions, but the irreducibly physical nature of the work keeps AI exposure very low.

title: Substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally built on human therapeutic relationships, empathy, and real-time interpersonal interaction, which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with documentation, treatment plan drafting, and administrative tasks, but the core counseling work — building trust, active listening, crisis intervention, and guiding behavioral change — requires human presence and connection that clients specifically seek. The strong projected job growth (17%) reflects sustained demand for human counselors, though AI tools may modestly improve productivity in documentation and resource referral tasks.

title: Surgical assistants and technologists
exposure_rationale: Surgical assistants and technologists perform highly physical, hands-on work in operating rooms — passing instruments, maintaining sterile fields, positioning patients, and directly assisting surgeons during procedures. These tasks require physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time responsiveness in unpredictable environments that AI cannot replicate. While robotic surgical systems exist, they augment surgeons rather than replace the supporting roles of technologists, and the physical, safety-critical nature of the work creates a strong barrier to AI displacement.

title: Survey researchers
exposure_rationale: Survey research is predominantly knowledge work done on a computer — designing surveys, analyzing data with statistical software, interpreting results, and writing reports — all tasks where AI is rapidly capable. The BLS already projects employment decline driven by big data and data mining replacing traditional survey methods, a trend AI will accelerate further. While some human judgment is needed for survey design and client communication, the core analytical and methodological tasks are highly automatable, placing this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

title: Surveying and mapping technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork (visiting sites, operating instruments, navigating terrain) and digital/knowledge work (GIS data processing, CAD software, map creation). AI and automation (drones, improved sensors) are already streamlining data collection, and AI tools can increasingly assist with image processing, map updating, and data integration. However, the significant physical presence required for fieldwork, equipment operation, and site verification creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement, keeping exposure moderate rather than high.

title: Surveyors
exposure_rationale: Surveying is a hybrid occupation with significant fieldwork requiring physical presence, travel to remote locations, and hands-on measurement in unpredictable terrain, which limits AI's direct substitution. However, the office-based components — processing GPS/GIS data, preparing maps and legal documents, analyzing records, and using CAD software — are increasingly automatable, and drone/remote sensing technologies are already noted as limiting employment growth. AI can substantially augment productivity in data interpretation and mapping tasks, but the licensed, legally accountable, and physically present nature of boundary certification creates a meaningful barrier to full displacement.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
exposure_rationale: Tax examination and revenue work is predominantly knowledge-based — reviewing financial documents, analyzing tax returns, interpreting regulations, and making determinations — all of which are tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI can already automate much of the routine return review, anomaly detection, and compliance checking that forms the core of this work. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as negotiating with taxpayers, conducting field audits, exercising judgment in complex cases, and navigating sensitive interpersonal situations with delinquent taxpayers, which moderates the score somewhat.

title: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical — driving a vehicle through real-world environments — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation in the near term. However, autonomous vehicle technology (Waymo, Tesla, etc.) represents a long-term existential threat that could eventually displace most of these workers, making the indirect AI exposure significant. The BLS still projects strong growth through 2034, suggesting the transition is not imminent, but the trajectory of self-driving technology means this occupation faces more AI-driven disruption than most physical jobs.

title: Teacher assistants
exposure_rationale: Teacher assistants perform predominantly in-person, hands-on work including supervising students, providing physical assistance to special needs students, and building interpersonal relationships — all of which require physical presence and human connection. While AI could assist with some peripheral tasks like recordkeeping or grading, the core duties of one-on-one student support, behavioral guidance, and physical care are highly resistant to automation. The occupation's projected slight decline likely reflects budget pressures more than AI displacement, and the irreplaceable human element in working with children and students with disabilities keeps AI exposure low.

title: Technical writers
exposure_rationale: Technical writing is almost entirely digital knowledge work — researching, drafting, editing, and structuring text-based documentation — which sits squarely in AI's strongest domain. LLMs are already capable of generating first drafts of manuals, how-to guides, and FAQs, and can rapidly synthesize technical specifications into plain language. The BLS itself notes that AI tools are slowing employment growth by boosting individual productivity, and the projected 1% growth over a decade reflects this displacement pressure, with the occupation's small size making even modest AI-driven efficiency gains highly impactful.

title: Telecommunications technicians
exposure_rationale: Telecommunications technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing towers, splicing fiber optic cables, installing equipment in homes and central offices — which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with remote diagnostics, network monitoring, and troubleshooting guidance, but the core job requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and on-site problem-solving. The occupation is already projected to decline slightly due to technological improvements in infrastructure durability rather than AI displacement specifically.

title: Tellers
exposure_rationale: Bank teller work is already being heavily disrupted by digital banking, ATMs, and automation — the BLS projects a 13% employment decline over the next decade. The core tasks (processing transactions, verifying accounts, handling cash) are increasingly handled by ATMs, mobile banking apps, and AI-driven systems, while the remaining human role shifts toward complex customer service interactions. The job has a physical/interpersonal component that provides some barrier, but the transaction-processing and information-handling aspects are highly automatable, and AI-enhanced kiosks and virtual tellers are already reducing headcount per branch.

title: Top executives
exposure_rationale: Top executives perform substantial knowledge work — analyzing reports, formulating strategy, and communicating decisions — that AI can increasingly assist with, pushing exposure upward. However, the core value of executive roles lies in human judgment, relationship-building, accountability, negotiation, and leadership in unpredictable organizational contexts, which AI cannot replicate. AI will likely make executives more productive by automating analysis and drafting tasks, but the interpersonal, political, and trust-based dimensions of leadership create a meaningful ceiling on displacement.

title: Tour and travel guides
exposure_rationale: Tour and travel guides work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — leading groups through locations, adapting to real-time situations, and building rapport with clients in person. While AI could assist with research, itinerary planning, and logistics, the core value of the job lies in live human presence, storytelling, and dynamic interaction that AI cannot replicate in the field. Some peripheral tasks like reservation management or content research may be streamlined by AI, but the occupation's growth trajectory and inherently embodied nature limit overall exposure.

title: Training and development managers
exposure_rationale: Training and development managers do predominantly knowledge work — designing curricula, assessing needs, managing budgets, and communicating with stakeholders — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the role involves substantial human elements: managing staff, building relationships with department leaders, facilitating live training, and exercising organizational judgment that requires contextual understanding of company culture. AI can accelerate content creation, needs analysis, and program evaluation, but the interpersonal leadership and organizational navigation components provide a meaningful buffer against full automation.

title: Training and development specialists
exposure_rationale: Training and development specialists do substantial knowledge work — designing curricula, creating learning materials, assessing needs, and evaluating programs — all areas where AI can meaningfully assist or partially automate. However, a significant portion of the role involves live facilitation, interpersonal coaching, and collaborative delivery that requires human presence and relationship-building. AI can accelerate content creation and needs analysis but is unlikely to fully replace the instructional and human-connection aspects of the job in the near term.

title: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers
exposure_rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (budgeting, planning, compliance monitoring, supply chain optimization) with substantial physical presence requirements (warehouse oversight, staff management, facility inspections) and real-time operational decision-making. AI can meaningfully assist with route optimization, inventory forecasting, compliance tracking, and logistics analytics, but the core managerial functions—supervising workers, resolving on-the-ground disruptions, negotiating with suppliers, and leading teams—still require human judgment and physical presence. The growing complexity of e-commerce supply chains may actually increase demand for these managers even as AI tools enhance their productivity.

title: Travel agents
exposure_rationale: Travel agents work primarily on computers and phones, researching destinations, booking reservations, and communicating with clients — all tasks where AI is already highly capable. AI-powered travel planning tools, chatbots, and booking platforms can automate much of the information-gathering, itinerary-building, and reservation-making that defines this role. However, the occupation retains some resilience through personalized client relationships, handling complex or emergency situations, and the value of firsthand destination experience that clients specifically seek out from human agents.

title: Tutors
exposure_rationale: Tutoring involves significant human interaction, relationship-building, and adaptive instruction that AI cannot fully replicate, particularly for students with disabilities or those needing emotional support and motivation. However, the BLS itself notes that generative AI will reduce demand by automating many core tutoring tasks like explaining concepts, providing practice problems, and giving feedback — all fundamentally digital knowledge-transfer activities. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level because while the interpersonal and adaptive elements provide some buffer, the core academic content delivery is highly susceptible to AI substitution.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and real-time, requiring officials to be present at sporting events, make split-second judgments based on direct observation, and manage interpersonal disputes with athletes and coaches. While AI and video replay technology can assist with some calls (e.g., hawk-eye systems, goal-line technology), the core job requires physical presence, situational awareness, and human authority that AI cannot replace. AI may incrementally assist with replay review but is unlikely to displace the need for on-field human officials in the foreseeable future.

title: Urban and regional planners
exposure_rationale: Urban and regional planners do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, GIS mapping, report writing, feasibility assessments — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, pushing exposure upward. However, a significant portion of the job involves stakeholder engagement, public meetings, political negotiation, and site visits that require human presence and interpersonal judgment. AI tools can meaningfully accelerate the analytical and documentation tasks, but the community-facing, regulatory, and relationship-management dimensions create a natural ceiling on full automation.

title: Veterinarians
exposure_rationale: Veterinarians perform substantial physical, hands-on work including surgery, wound treatment, physical examinations, and operating medical equipment that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with diagnostic support, medical record analysis, treatment planning, and interpreting imaging results, making the knowledge-work portions of the job more efficient. The core of the occupation remains grounded in physical presence, manual dexterity, and direct animal care, creating a natural ceiling on AI's ability to reshape the role fundamentally.

title: Veterinary assistants and laboratory animal caretakers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly physical and hands-on — feeding, bathing, restraining, and caring for animals in person — with essentially no core tasks that can be digitized or automated by AI. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, or monitoring systems, but these are peripheral to the job's primary duties. The high injury rate and physical demands underscore how deeply rooted this work is in real-world, unpredictable animal interactions.

title: Veterinary technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: Veterinary technologists and technicians perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — restraining animals, administering anesthesia, collecting samples, providing nursing care — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity in unpredictable environments. AI may assist with diagnostic image analysis or record-keeping on the periphery, but the core job involves direct animal handling and real-time clinical judgment that cannot be automated. The occupation's high injury rate and physical demands underscore its fundamentally non-digital nature.

title: Waiters and waitresses
exposure_rationale: Waiting tables is fundamentally a physical, in-person job requiring carrying food, reading customer moods, and providing hospitality — tasks AI cannot directly perform. Some peripheral tasks like order-taking are being automated via kiosks and self-service technology, which explains the slight projected employment decline, but the core human interaction and physical service elements remain resistant to AI replacement. AI has minimal impact on the daily work of actually serving customers in a dining environment.

title: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators
exposure_rationale: This occupation involves significant physical presence, hands-on equipment operation, chemical handling, and on-site inspections that cannot be done remotely or digitally. However, AI and automation are already impacting the monitoring and control aspects — computerized systems increasingly manage treatment processes, contributing to the projected 7% employment decline. The core job still requires skilled workers physically present to respond to emergencies, maintain equipment, and operate complex systems, limiting AI exposure to moderate levels.

title: Water transportation workers
exposure_rationale: Water transportation work is fundamentally physical and place-bound, requiring workers to be aboard vessels operating in dynamic, unpredictable maritime environments. While AI could assist with navigation planning, route optimization, and some monitoring tasks, the core duties — operating vessels, maintaining machinery, managing cargo and passengers in real-time, and responding to emergencies at sea — require physical presence and hands-on skill that AI cannot replace. Autonomous shipping technology exists in early stages but faces enormous regulatory, safety, and technical barriers, keeping human crews essential for the foreseeable future.

title: Web developers and digital designers
exposure_rationale: Web development and digital design are almost entirely computer-based occupations where core tasks — writing code, designing interfaces, creating graphics, and testing applications — are precisely the domains where AI is advancing most rapidly. AI tools like GitHub Copilot, generative design platforms, and no-code/low-code builders are already substantially augmenting or replacing routine coding and design tasks, meaning fewer workers can produce the same output. While client communication, creative judgment, and complex problem-solving provide some buffer, the fundamentally digital nature of the work and the steep AI capability trajectory in coding and design place this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

title: Welders, cutters, solderers, and brazers
exposure_rationale: Welding is fundamentally a hands-on, physical trade requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and real-time judgment in variable environments — qualities that AI cannot replicate directly. While robotic welding automation exists in manufacturing, this affects the broader industry rather than representing AI reshaping the cognitive or knowledge work of skilled welders. AI might assist peripherally with blueprint interpretation or quality inspection, but the core job remains physical and resistant to AI displacement.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
exposure_rationale: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives blend significant interpersonal, relationship-driven work (client visits, negotiations, trust-building) with digital tasks like order processing, reporting, and communication that AI can readily assist with or automate. AI tools such as CRM automation, chatbots, and lead-generation platforms are already encroaching on inside sales functions, while outside sales roles retain a human advantage in complex negotiations and relationship management. The BLS itself notes that AI and automation may limit employment growth, but the physical travel, face-to-face selling, and nuanced client relationship aspects provide a meaningful buffer against full AI displacement.

title: Wind turbine technicians
exposure_rationale: Wind turbine technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing 200-foot towers, replacing mechanical components, lubricating parts, and rappelling to service blades — all of which require physical presence and cannot be done remotely by AI. AI might assist with remote monitoring, diagnostics, and maintenance scheduling, but the core job requires a human body in a physically demanding and often hazardous environment. The digital/analytical components (using computers to diagnose electrical malfunctions, collecting turbine data) are a minor fraction of the overall role.

title: Woodworkers
exposure_rationale: Woodworking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and direct interaction with materials and machinery. While CNC machines and computer-aided design already automate some aspects of the work, the core tasks — cutting, shaping, assembling, finishing, and installing wood products — require physical presence and skilled manual labor that AI cannot directly replace. AI may marginally assist with design interpretation or machine programming, but the occupation's primary barriers to automation are physical, not informational.

title: Writers and authors
exposure_rationale: Writing and authorship is almost entirely a digital, knowledge-based occupation where the core work product — text — is precisely what modern AI systems excel at generating, editing, and refining. AI tools like large language models can already draft articles, blog posts, ad copy, scripts, and other content at scale, directly threatening the volume of work available to human writers. While originality, deep subject expertise, unique voice, and human relationships with editors/clients provide some buffer, the fundamental task of producing written content is highly automatable, and many lower-tier writing jobs (copywriting, content writing, blogging) are already being displaced.

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
exposure_rationale: Zoologists and wildlife biologists split their time between physical fieldwork (collecting specimens, observing animals in remote habitats, handling wildlife) and knowledge work (data analysis, writing reports, GIS modeling, conservation planning). AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and writing components — species identification from images, population modeling, literature synthesis — but the core fieldwork requiring physical presence in unpredictable natural environments creates a substantial barrier to AI displacement. The occupation's heavy reliance on outdoor skills, direct animal observation, and hands-on data collection keeps overall AI exposure moderate.
